claims.text: Ed and Lorraine Warren — the basis for the Conjuring film franchise — present themselves as the most prolific paranormal investigators in history with more than 10,000 cases on which the Conjuring films were directly based.

evidence.quote:
Ed and Lorraine Warren are maybe the
most famous paranormal investigators in
history they've worked on more than 10
000 cases some of which have been made
into books and movies The Conjuring
films are based on the work of the
warrants today we'll look at a few of
their scariest most disturbing cases so
turn off the lights and put the little
ones to bed it's gonna get spooky

claims.rationale: The Warrens are real, were New England-based self-styled paranormal investigators, and the Conjuring franchise (Warner Bros.) is built on their case lore — these facts are accurate. The 10,000-cases figure is the Warrens' own claim, which the host explicitly debunks later in the verdict section by computing it would require 27 years of one case per day. The framing claim is true at the surface level (the films cite their case files); the underlying number is exaggerated.

claims.text: The 1975 Amityville haunting story holds that George and Kathleen Lutz fled 112 Ocean Avenue after 28 days of demonic activity in the house where Ronald 'Butch' DeFeo had murdered his entire family the year before, supposedly hearing a voice tell their priest to 'get out,' seeing hoof prints in the snow, and George waking at 3:15 AM — the exact time of the DeFeo murders.

the insanity defense but it didn't work
in November 1975 whi

ch was sentenced to

like this

strange activity at first it was just

the heat so they kept the fire going day
and night George sometimes saw demonic
faces in the fire Missy Lutz then five

was found oozing from the walls and
through keyholes doors and windows were

claims.rationale: The DeFeo murders (Nov 13, 1974) and the Lutz family's brief 28-day stay in early 1976 are real historical events. The supernatural elements (slamming doors, voice telling priest to leave, hoof prints in snow, 3:15 AM paralysis, levitating wife) are the Lutz/Anson book account — and as the host explicitly debunks later in the episode, weather records show no snow, the house had no damage when they left, and Father Pecoraro publicly disputed many claims. The Lutz lawyer William Weber later said the story was concocted over wine. So the host presents the claim then debunks it himself.

claims.text: The Annabelle case has nursing student Donna's Raggedy Ann doll, supposedly possessed by the spirit of a girl named Annabelle Higgins, escalating to attacking Lou with seven scratch marks 'in the shape of the mark of the beast' before Ed and Lorraine Warren took it to live in a glass case at their occult museum.

evidence.quote:
1970 Donna a nursing student received a
gift from her mother for her 20th
birthday a Raggedy Ann doll Donna loved
the doll and took it back to her house
that she shared with another young nurse
named Angie the doll's place in the
house was sitting up on the couch but
Donna and Angie noticed the doll would
be found in different rooms Donna would
set her on the living room couch before
she left work she'd come home and the
doll was in her room with the door shut
then Donna and Angie started finding
notes around the apartment they were
written in child's handwriting and said
help me and the notes were written on
parchment paper that they didn't have in

doll had moved again but this time it
had a sticky red substance on its hands
that appeared to be blood within a few

creepy feeling and told him to get rid
of it they wouldn't that night while Lou
was sleeping he heard a noise in the
room like someone was walking around
then he felt something on the bed he
looked up and said the dawn was quickly
crawling on the bed on his body he went
to yell but the doll covered his mouth
and choked him until he passed out the
next morning he tried to convince
himself that it was all a dream but he
couldn't shake it Donna and Angie didn't
know what to do they felt like they were
going crazy they contacted a local

and told the women that the doll was
possessed by a seven-year-old girl named

claims.rationale: The Annabelle doll exists in the Warrens' Occult Museum and the Warrens did claim to take possession of a Raggedy Ann doll from two nurses around 1970. The supernatural elements have no independent witnesses. The host debunks the case later by showing it's substantially identical to the 1963 Twilight Zone episode 'Living Doll' — which featured a doll named in a way the show's stepmother shared a name with the eventual Annabelle character. There is no contemporaneous newspaper report of any of the events.

claims.text: The Perron family case (basis for The Conjuring, 2013) has the witch Bathsheba Sherman tormenting Carolyn Perron at the family's Harrisville, Rhode Island farmhouse and trying to make her kill her own children — until the Warrens arrived, performed a seance against Roger's wishes, and Carolyn went into a violent trance before Roger punched Ed and threw the Warrens out.

January 1971 the Perrin family

claims.rationale: The Perron family did live in the Harrisville farmhouse from 1971-1980 and Andrea Perron has written a memoir about supposed hauntings; the house exists and Bathsheba Sherman was a real woman who lived nearby. The host debunks the supernatural account in the verdict using current owner Norma Sutcliffe's video: Bathsheba never lived in that house, never killed anyone, was by all accounts a kind woman who died at 73 leaving money for grandchildren's education. The seance and Roger Perron punching Ed Warren is the Perron/Warren account — supported by Roger's own audio testimony quoted in the transcript.

claims.text: The 1977 Enfield Poltergeist case in North London produced an official police-report observation of a chair sliding across the floor, the voice of an old man named 'Bill' speaking through 11-year-old Janet Hodgson — describing his own death from a brain hemorrhage in a chair downstairs, later confirmed by Bill Wilkins's son — and over 30 witnesses to physical phenomena, with paranormal investigator Guy Playfair spending the most time documenting it.

evidence.quote:
August 1977 Enfield North London this is
considered one of the events that proves
the existence of poltergeists Peggy
Hodgson had just put her children to bed
when she heard the sound of a chair
being dragged across the bedroom floor

daughters were under their blankets
terrified the chair was upside down on
the floor and they said they didn't do
it they said the chair moved on its own

wobble then she saw the chair slide

weren't moving this voice is coming from

and parapsychologist with the society
for psychical research he spent a great
deal

claims.rationale: The Enfield case (1977-1979) is real and famous; WPC Carolyn Heeps did file a report describing seeing a chair move (her account is on record). Bill Wilkins's voice through Janet was confirmed in detail by his son Terry. Guy Lyon Playfair (SPR) and Maurice Grosse spent over a year on it. However the host then notes correctly that Janet later admitted some of the activity was faked, the children were caught faking, and the famous 'levitation photo' looks like jumping. So while the case has real eyewitness reports including police, it has substantial admitted hoaxing — overall it's mixed evidence at best.

claims.text: The Snedeker case has the family discovering in 1986 that their rented Connecticut house was a former Hallahan funeral home — with embalming equipment and a casket display room in the basement — and being terrorized by violent ghosts including sexual assaults on Carmen and her niece, until the Warrens performed an exorcism after nine weeks of investigation.

claims.rationale: The Snedeker family's rental of the former Hallahan Funeral Home in Southington, CT in 1986 is documented. The supernatural account is the basis of the 'A Haunting in Connecticut' book and 2009 film. The host devastatingly debunks it later: ghostwriter Ray Garton has on record stated that Ed Warren told him to 'use what works and just make the rest up,' the family had documented drug and alcohol problems, the upstairs tenant saw nothing, son Philip had untreated schizophrenia, and the haunting reports started after eviction proceedings began. The supernatural elements are not credible.

claims.text: In the verdict, the host concludes the Amityville Horror is fully fabricated — a story William Weber, the DeFeo lawyer, openly admits was invented over four bottles of wine with the Lutzes as a joint scheme to fund a defense book and a Lutz book deal, with author Jay Anson then offering the Lutzes a 50/50 split that locked them into the story.

evidence.quote:
couldn't have seen hoof prints in the
snow because there was no snow at that
time and all the damage that supposedly
happened to the house didn't doors
windows the entire house was in perfect
shape when they moved in and when they
left the Amityville story was actually
created by Butch defeo's lawyer William
Weber and the Lott's family one night
they were discussing a way to help each
other Weber wanted an insanity verdict
for Butch defeo he was also working on a
book deal the lutzes were struggling
dealing financially and

claims.rationale: Weber did publicly state in interviews (most prominently 1979) that he and the Lutzes 'created this horror story over many bottles of wine.' Independent debunkers (Stephen Kaplan, Father Pecoraro, weather records) have demolished the Amityville claims. The film/book franchise persists despite the original story being widely understood as fabricated. The host's framing of this as 'definitely debunked' is consistent with mainstream skeptical literature.

dependencies.rationale: This verdict directly contradicts the supernatural Amityville account presented in c_0002.

claims.text: The Annabelle story is essentially a beat-for-beat retelling of the 1963 Twilight Zone episode 'Living Doll,' which aired on CBS and even shares the name 'Annabelle' for the mother — strong evidence the Warrens copied the plot rather than investigated a real haunting.

evidence.quote:
The Amityville
Horror Story is definitely debunked the
next case we talked about was Annabelle
the possessed doll this story is easier
to track it's basically a retelling beat
for Beat of The Twilight Zone episode
living doll which aired in 1963 on CBS
here's the synopsis Eric Streeter is

another doll Christy loves her new

going to get rid of you

the mother's name in The Twilight Zone
episode was Annabelle we can call that
one debunked how about

claims.rationale: 'Living Doll' did air on Twilight Zone in 1963 (S5E6) starring Telly Savalas as a stepfather menaced by a Talky Tina doll. Whether the mother's character is named Annabelle and whether the structural similarity is real beat-for-beat copying is debatable — the mother in 'Living Doll' is named Annabelle Streator, which the host accurately notes. The host's argument is suggestive but doesn't rule out independent later coincidence. Reasonable but not airtight inference.

dependencies.rationale: Casts the Annabelle case as plagiarized fiction rather than investigated haunting.

claims.text: Bathsheba Sherman was a real but apparently kind woman who never lived in the Perron farmhouse, never killed anyone, died peacefully at 73 leaving money for her grandchildren's education — facts established by current owner Norma Sutcliffe, who was so harassed by Conjuring fans that she sued Warner Bros.

claims.rationale: Bathsheba Sherman was a real Rhode Island woman who died in 1885 at age 72 (close to 73). She lived on a different farm and was never connected to the Perron house, was never accused of witchcraft in her lifetime, and has descendants who have publicly objected to her portrayal in The Conjuring. Norma Sutcliffe did sue Warner Bros. in 2015. The host's debunking is broadly accurate.

dependencies.rationale: Refutes the witch identity at the heart of the Perron/Conjuring narrative.

claims.rationale: Ray Garton's 'use what works and make the rest up' quote is well-documented and he has reaffirmed it many times in interviews and in print, openly disavowing 'In a Dark Place' as a true story. He also says he never actually saw Philip in person. The Snedekers' financial troubles and Philip's mental illness are documented. The 'started after eviction proceedings' and 'tenant upstairs heard nothing' details are well-attested. This is the strongest debunking in the episode.

dependencies.rationale: Demolishes the Snedeker case account given in c_0006.

claims.text: Ed Warren himself initiated a sexual relationship with Judith Penny when she was 15 — a girl who took his bus — and continued it for over 40 years with Lorraine's knowledge, with Judith living in the Warrens' house in an apartment built for her, becoming pregnant by Ed in 1978; Judith also alleges Ed regularly beat Lorraine unconscious.

claims.rationale: Judith Penny's sworn declaration in connection with Tony Spera (the Warrens' son-in-law) and the Conjuring litigation is real and was reported by The Hollywood Reporter and other outlets in 2014. The Warner Bros. Conjuring contract did reportedly contain unusual restrictions on depicting an affair with a minor, lending the allegation circumstantial corroboration. The Spera family disputes the account. Penny was reportedly paid $150,000 by WB. The allegations are credible and serious but uncontested in court (Warren died in 2006).

claims.text: The host's overall verdict is that no Warrens case is supported by evidence beyond their own word, that Ed manufactured 'evidence' (Judith Penny was instructed by Ed to put on a white dress and walk a cemetery to produce the famous 'Lady in White' video), and that because Ed lied about some things, his entire body of paranormal claims must be doubted.

claims.rationale: The host's epistemic framing — no independent evidence, only the Warrens' word; documented fabrication of at least one piece of evidence (the Lady in White video, per Judith Penny's account); therefore credibility is undermined for everything else — is a sound application of inductive skepticism. Skeptical analysts (Joe Nickell, Steven Novella) have made the same case for decades. The 'self-proclaimed demonologist' barb has factual basis: Ed had no theological credentials.

dependencies.rationale: The Judith Penny revelations are part of why the host concludes Ed's word can't be trusted on the cases.
